rows.sat_rat: Design keying and post-change flow/leak checks catch many misseats, but unsealed filters still slip through when verification is skipped. Seating and checking a filter is fast, and post-install leak checks add only minutes. Correct seating depends on technician care and feel, and misseating happens with rushed or inexperienced staff. Filters and gaskets are inexpensive, though an unsealed filter can invalidate costly samples.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of air samplers dropping out during coordinated collection
rows.plain: Reduce how often air-sampling devices stop working or disconnect while collecting samples together. This helps ensure the area is monitored properly.
rows.imp_rat: Dropping out during coordinated collection undermines multi-point sampling integrity, which is central to the job; the consequence is invalidated comparative data though sometimes partial recovery is possible.
rows.sat_rat: Without networked status feedback, individual samplers dropping out during coordinated collection often go unnoticed until download, undermining synchronized results. Coordinated multi-sampler runs are launched together but require monitoring across units, adding oversight time. Coordinating multiple samplers and detecting a dropout mid-run requires attentive judgement across the network. A single unit dropping out can invalidate the whole coordinated dataset, wasting all units' consumables and the campaign effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sampling-path leaks caused by improperly seated replacement components
rows.plain: Prevent leaks in the sample-taking route when replacement parts are not fitted correctly. Properly seated parts help keep samples accurate and prevent spills or loss.
rows.imp_rat: Sampling-path leaks from improperly seated components corrupt flow and capture accuracy, core to the job; severity is high because leaked air produces unrepresentative, invalid results.
rows.sat_rat: Flow-rate verification and leak checks after reassembly are standard and catch most seating leaks, though slow or intermittent leaks can escape. Post-service leak testing adds a short verification step to the maintenance cycle. Proper seating of components to avoid leaks relies on technician skill and torque discipline. Leak-test equipment is a modest fixed cost and reruns from undetected leaks waste resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between documented sampler settings and settings used for collection
rows.plain: Make the equipment settings written in the records match the settings actually used when samples were collected. This helps others understand and trust the test results.
rows.imp_rat: A discrepancy between documented and used sampler settings can invalidate sampled volume, flow, or recovery assumptions, making relevance very high; severity is high because apparently valid records may support incorrect contamination conclusions.
rows.sat_rat: Stored methods, access controls, and automated audit trails usually align documented and actual settings, with errors concentrated in older manually configured equipment. Saved methods, pre-use verification, and electronic run records make confirming sampler settings fast during line setup. Locked recipes and guided interfaces limit configuration expertise, although operators must still select the correct method and review displayed values. Configuration controls have little incremental operating cost and prevent waste from invalid collections and investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from required sample-handling conditions during transfer
rows.plain: Keep collected samples under the required conditions while they are being moved. This helps ensure the samples remain suitable for testing.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from required transfer conditions can change organism survival or sample integrity, so relevance to viable quantification is high; severity is high because the resulting count may be invalid or misleading.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized containers, temperature controls, and chain-of-custody procedures serve most transfers well, but manual handoffs leave recurring gaps. Defined transfer windows and established routes usually keep handling conditions within limits, though queues and delayed pickups can extend transfer time. Procedures, training, and labeled transport containers make the task routine, but operators must still recognize and respond to condition excursions. Controlled carriers and monitoring supplies impose modest costs compared with the material and investigation costs of a compromised sample.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that airflow fails to start when the operator initiates the sampling run
rows.plain: Make sure the airflow starts when the operator begins collecting an air sample. Without airflow, the sample may not be collected properly.
rows.imp_rat: If airflow fails to start when initiated, no sample is collected at all — a direct failure of the core job — though the error is immediately visible so the technician can typically retry, limiting downstream severity.
rows.sat_rat: Startup failures are uncommon with maintained pumps and give an immediate visible/audible fault, so the operator catches them on the spot. Startup confirmation is near-instant, with pumps reaching flow within seconds of initiation. Little skill is required as most units self-confirm flow and display a fault if the pump does not spin up. A false start wastes only a repeat setup, and the pump hardware itself is inexpensive to keep serviceable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the collection medium during sampling run initiation
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted material from getting into the sample container when sampling begins. This helps ensure the sample accurately represents what is being tested.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the collection medium at run initiation compromises the sample from the start (high relevance); the affected sample must be discarded and the run repeated (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile pre-packaged media and aseptic loading practice keep initiation contamination fairly low, though the exposed handling moment remains a residual risk. Loading media at initiation is a quick step but must be done carefully, adding modest time. Aseptically loading the medium at run start relies on operator technique and is a common contamination point. A contaminated medium at start wastes the sample and the consumable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish area during sampler repair
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or germs from entering the clean medicine-filling area while a sampling device is being repaired. This protects the cleanliness of the production process.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during repair is somewhat off the routine sampling path (moderate relevance), but breaching a sterile fill-finish area during service can contaminate product and trigger catastrophic recalls (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature aseptic-intervention SOPs and environmental monitoring make contamination introduction uncommon, though every human entry into the sterile zone carries residual risk. Gowning, line clearance, and aseptic repair procedures lengthen any intervention in the fill-finish area but are strictly scheduled. Aseptic repair in a Grade A/B zone demands highly trained personnel with rigorous cleanroom discipline, a scarce competency. Cleanroom entry, gowning consumables, and potential line-clearance downtime make each repair costly.

rows.source_job: Load collection medium into air sampler
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the collection medium is installed in the wrong orientation or not fully seated in the collection chamber
rows.plain: Make sure the sampling material is facing the right way and is fully in place inside the air-sampling chamber. Otherwise, the air sample may be unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong media orientation or improper seating can defeat collection efficiency, directly harming the core job (high relevance); it invalidates the affected sample but is often catchable on inspection (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Keyed fittings and visual cues help but ambiguous designs still allow reversed or unseated media, a recurring operator error. Correctly seating and orienting the medium adds little time when done right. Proper orientation and full seating depend entirely on operator attention and familiarity with the specific chamber design. A misseated medium can invalidate a sample, wasting media and requiring a re-run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between sampler position and the designated sampling point
rows.plain: Keep the sampler in the exact place chosen for taking the sample. This helps ensure the sample represents the intended location.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from the designated sampling point undermines the representativeness of collected air, making it highly relevant; severity is high because contamination at the required location may remain undetected and the result may not support release decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified locations and repeatable fixtures generally produce consistent placement, with residual deviations arising from setup variation or equipment interference. Position marks, photographs, fixtures, and setup checklists allow the sampler to be placed at the designated point without lengthy measurement. Clear location maps and mechanical positioning aids reduce judgement, although constrained fill-line geometry can still require operator finesse. Reusable brackets and visual references control position with little recurring cost, but custom mounts and requalification add occasional expense.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the collected sample becomes contaminated while ending the run
rows.plain: Keep the collected sample clean when the sampling process is stopped. This helps ensure the sample still accurately represents what was collected.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination while ending the run corrupts the finished sample just before capture (high relevance), invalidating an otherwise complete result and requiring rework (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Immediate sealing and capping practice keeps end-of-run contamination low, though the exposure during removal is a residual vulnerability. Retrieving and sealing the medium at run end is quick but must be handled with care. Aseptic removal and sealing at run end depend on operator handling skill. End-of-run contamination wastes the completed sample and its analysis cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of moisture lost from the sample-bearing medium before incubation
rows.plain: Keep the material holding the sample from drying out before testing begins. This helps preserve the sample in its original condition.
rows.imp_rat: Moisture loss from the sample-bearing medium can reduce organism recovery and distort counts, making it highly relevant; severity is high but generally manifests as compromised data or a repeat-sampling need.
rows.sat_rat: Covered plates and controlled storage usually preserve media moisture, yet prolonged sampling, strong airflow, and delayed incubation can still cause meaningful loss. Covers, sealed bags, and rapid transport reduce the time during which agar is exposed to drying conditions. Operators need basic handling discipline rather than specialized expertise, although recognizing excessive desiccation can be subjective. Protective packaging is inexpensive, but dried plates may be discarded or repeated at additional material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sampler stops before a scheduled cycle is complete
rows.plain: Make sure the sampling device keeps running until its planned cycle is finished. This prevents missing part of the unattended sample.
rows.imp_rat: A sampler stopping before a scheduled cycle completes produces a truncated, non-representative sample central to the job's failure; consequences include lost monitoring windows and re-work, though often detectable.
rows.sat_rat: Power loss, battery exhaustion, overheating or mechanical faults still cause intermittent mid-cycle stops that undermine unattended confidence. Scheduled cycles run autonomously, saving operator attendance time. Once programmed the cycle needs no skilled oversight, but recovering from a mid-cycle stop requires troubleshooting judgement. An aborted cycle wastes the collection medium and mandates a rerun, incurring labor and consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual pharmaceutical or biological contamination makes the air sampler unsafe to transfer
rows.plain: Make sure no leftover medicine or germs make an air-sampling device unsafe to move or give to someone else. The device should be clean and safe before it is transferred.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pharmaceutical or biological contamination making the unit unsafe to transfer is tangential to collecting the sample itself but has severe consequences for technician safety and cross-contamination of clean areas.
rows.sat_rat: Decon protocols exist but confidence that residuals are truly gone is inconsistent, so units are sometimes transferred with unverified status. Decontamination and swab-test turnaround adds days before a unit is cleared for transfer, slowing the disposal path. Assessing residual pharma/biological burden requires microbiology and EHS judgement that not every disposal crew has on hand. Wipe-down, VHP or autoclave decon plus verification testing consume reagents and lab time on each unit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of protective airflow disruption caused by air sampling at critical locations
rows.plain: Disturb the protective airflow as little as possible when taking air samples in important locations. This helps keep the area protected while it is being checked.
rows.imp_rat: Disrupting protective airflow at critical locations means sampling itself endangers first-air protection where the CFJ operates (solid relevance); airflow disruption over product can cause contamination of critical exposures (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Airflow visualization and qualification practices usually identify disruptive configurations, but sampler geometry, tubing, and operator presence can still alter localized protection. Compact samplers, remote heads, and airflow studies allow placement decisions without excessive delay, though site-specific smoke-study confirmation can be time-consuming. Assessing wake effects and first-air interference requires trained airflow specialists and careful operator technique at critical locations. Low-profile equipment and existing airflow-visualization programs often control disruption without major incremental cost, while remote or integrated systems cost more.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of terminating airflow before the designated collection endpoint
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of stopping the airflow before the sample has reached the required collection point. This helps ensure the sample run is completed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Terminating airflow before the designated endpoint yields an incomplete sample volume, directly defeating accurate collection; the shortfall can invalidate the run and require costly re-sampling.
rows.sat_rat: Battery depletion, power interruptions and fault trips can cut a run short before the designated endpoint, and unattended units may not be noticed until download. Endpoint timing is handled by internal timers so no operator time is spent tracking it. Programmed run lengths remove most manual judgement, though correct setup still depends on the technician. Premature termination wastes the sampling event and any consumables, forcing a costly repeat visit or run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the collected sample during removal or handoff
rows.plain: Keep the collected sample clean while it is removed and handed to someone else. This helps ensure the sample accurately represents what was collected.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during sample removal or handoff compromises the biological result after collection and can create a false-positive excursion. This is highly relevant to result integrity and severe because it may drive costly investigations, batch impact, or loss of confidence in monitoring data.
rows.sat_rat: Standard closure and transfer practices make contamination during removal uncommon, but manual handling and damaged media containers remain failure points. Immediate covering, sealed carriers, and defined handoff steps allow samples to be removed promptly with little open exposure. Routine aseptic training supports consistent removal, although awkward access and glove manipulations still demand attention. Sterile lids, bags, and carriers add limited expense and avoid much larger costs from invalid or contaminated samples.

rows.source_job: Verify sampler operation at run start
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sampler airflow fails to stabilize at the required setpoint before site departure
rows.plain: Make sure the air sampler reaches and holds the needed airflow before leaving the work site, so the sample can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: If airflow fails to stabilize at setpoint before site departure, the unattended run proceeds at an incorrect rate for its duration, central to accurate collection and severe because no one is present to correct it.
rows.sat_rat: Most modern samplers stabilize quickly and show a ready indicator, so leaving before stabilization is now uncommon. The technician must wait for flow to stabilize before leaving, and slow-settling units extend the on-site dwell. Judging when flow has truly stabilized takes some experience on units without a settled-status indicator. Extra dwell time is mostly a labor cost with little material impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sampler inlet or internal flow path during filter replacement
rows.plain: Keep the sampler’s opening and inside air path clean while changing its protective filters. This helps prevent unwanted material from getting into the sampler.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the inlet or flow path during filter replacement biases subsequent samples via the sampler itself (moderately high relevance), and persistent flow-path contamination can corrupt multiple downstream runs before detection (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic filter-change SOPs and clean-bench work handle most cases, but inadvertent inlet or flow-path contamination during handling still occurs. Filter changes are quick but must be done carefully in a clean area, adding modest setup time. Clean filter replacement without touching sealing surfaces requires trained technique and attention, prone to error under time pressure. Filters are consumables and the added cleanliness measures are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating sampling surfaces while assembling the sampling head, stages, and intake components
rows.plain: Keep the surfaces used for collecting samples clean while putting together the sample-collecting parts. This helps prevent the samples from being spoiled by unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sampling surfaces during assembly of the head and intake components risks corrupting the sample before collection; moderately central and consequential when it produces spurious results.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-piece intakes and cascade stages have many touchable surfaces, so contamination during assembly is a frequent, poorly-mitigated failure. Careful contamination-free assembly of heads and stages is a slow, deliberate manual operation. Clean assembly of multi-stage sampling heads requires substantial aseptic dexterity and attention that few operators perform flawlessly. Surface contamination causes rejected runs and rework, wasting consumables and effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded sampled volume and air actually drawn across the pause
rows.plain: Keep the recorded sample amount close to the amount of air actually pulled in while the run is paused, so the sample record remains accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded volume and air drawn across a pause degrades quantitative accuracy of the sample (high relevance); the error affects data reliability but is typically limited to pause-handling edge cases (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Better controllers pause volume integration cleanly, but simpler units may keep counting or lose track across a pause, producing recorded-versus-actual mismatch. Pause handling is automatic on programmable units, adding no operator time. Reconciling volume across a pause requires trained interpretation of the flow log if the sampler does not auto-account for it. Volume mismatch across pauses can invalidate a sample and force a rerun.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contamination introduced into the classified area from the sampler exterior
rows.plain: Keep germs and other unwanted material on the outside of the sampling tool from getting into the controlled clean room. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced from the sampler exterior is somewhat ancillary to measurement but can compromise the environment being monitored, so relevance is high; severity is very high because it may contaminate exposed sterile product or critical surfaces.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning procedures and smooth sampler surfaces usually control exterior bioburden, with residual risk at crevices, interfaces, and inadequately wetted areas. Routine disinfection can be completed quickly with established wipe-down procedures before equipment enters classified areas. Validated disinfectants and standard wiping patterns reduce the expertise required, though complete surface coverage still depends on operator technique. Disinfectant, sterile wipes, and labor add modest recurring costs but are already standard cleanroom supplies.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, procurement officers, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel responsible for sampler selection.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected air sampler is incompatible with the target analytes or required collection method
rows.plain: Choose an air-sampling device that can detect the substances being checked and collect them in the required way. This helps ensure the test results are useful.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a sampler incompatible with the target analytes or method undermines the whole collection outcome at the planning stage; severe in wasted effort and invalid data, though typically caught during setup verification.
rows.sat_rat: Established sampling-method standards (EU GMP Annex 1, ISO 14698) and vendor selection guides make compatibility a well-covered decision, but non-standard analytes still cause occasional mismatches. Matching analytes to sampler type is a routine early-spec step that adds modest lead time when method requirements are known upfront. Microbiologists and QC engineers in pharma routinely map viable/non-viable and particle-collection methods to sampler types, though niche analytes still demand scarce specialist judgement. Getting this wrong forces re-procurement, so buyers generally invest in vetting compatibility rather than eating replacement costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a planned sampling location is inaccessible or obstructed when collection is due
rows.plain: Make sure the planned sampling point can be reached and is not blocked when it is time to collect a sample. This helps ensure the right sample is collected on time.
rows.imp_rat: An inaccessible or obstructed sampling location prevents collection at the required point, directly blocking the CFJ (relevance high); a missed representative location leaves a monitoring gap, moderately-to-highly severe.
rows.sat_rat: Predefined locations and scheduling help, but temporary equipment, hoses, carts, and active operations commonly block planned access. Obstructions discovered at collection time cause waiting, relocation, or coordination with production before sampling can proceed. Operators need situational awareness and communication skills to identify an acceptable alternative without compromising the approved plan. Inaccessible locations waste labor and may require specialized mounts, extensions, or facility modifications.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous contamination transfers from the air sampler to personnel or surrounding work areas during decontamination
rows.plain: Prevent harmful contamination from spreading from the air-collecting device to people or nearby work areas while it is being cleaned. This protects workers and keeps the surrounding area safe.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous contamination transferring off the sampler during decontamination sits outside the primary collection function (moderate relevance), but spreading hazardous agents to personnel or workspaces is a serious health and containment breach (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-established containment and PPE practices reliably protect personnel, with residual exposure risk mainly from procedural lapses. Donning PPE and using containment enclosures adds handling time but is standard for hazardous-sample work. Safe decontamination handling relies on trained biosafety/radiation-safety practice that established facilities generally possess. PPE, fume hoods or glove boxes, and waste handling add ongoing cost that facilities routinely budget for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sampler fails to maintain the required collection conditions during the blank run
rows.plain: Make sure the air-collecting device keeps the required conditions during a test run with no sample. This helps show whether later air-sample results can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to maintain required conditions during a blank run undermines the sampler's ability to prove baseline cleanliness (high relevance); a bad blank voids the validation control but is contained to that run (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Feedback-controlled pumps hold flow and conditions well in most units, though drift or fault during a blank run still occurs occasionally. The blank run itself takes the full programmed duration, which is unavoidable time. Modern samplers largely automate flow control, reducing the skill needed to hold conditions steady. A failed blank run wastes the run time and media, a moderate cost.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, procurement officers, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel responsible for sampler selection.
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of incompleteness or conflict in evidence that candidate air samplers meet applicable regulatory and sampling-method requirements
rows.plain: Make sure there is complete, consistent proof that possible air-collecting devices meet required rules and testing methods. This supports choosing equipment that is suitable for its intended use.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete evidence that samplers meet regulatory and method requirements is a selection-stage concern moderately central to the job; when it occurs, samples may be legally unusable, a significant but upstream consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor documentation is frequently patchy or contradictory, and no standard evidence package exists, so gaps and conflicts remain a common, only partially mitigated pain. Chasing and reconciling regulatory and method-compliance evidence from vendors is a slow, iterative back-and-forth. Interpreting whether vendor evidence actually satisfies applicable standards requires experienced regulatory/QA judgement that is in short supply. Incomplete evidence surfacing late drives costly requalification or rejected purchases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of incomparability among airborne microbial results from different classified zones
rows.plain: Make test results about airborne germs as comparable as possible between different controlled clean areas. This makes it easier to tell whether one area has more contamination than another.
rows.imp_rat: Incomparability among results from different classified zones impairs trending, benchmarking, and cross-zone interpretation but does not necessarily invalidate each individual collection. Its consequences are moderate because deterioration or migration patterns may be missed or misread.
rows.sat_rat: Results are commonly reported as colony-forming units per cubic meter, yet environmental and methodological differences prevent consistently like-for-like comparisons between zones. Standard reporting units and electronic trending aid comparison, but contextual normalization across grades, activities, volumes, and sampling methods still consumes analyst time. Experienced personnel must interpret differences in background bioburden, airflow, occupancy, and monitoring design before comparing zone results. Most facilities can compare existing data without major capital expense, although harmonizing systems and conducting bridging studies creates additional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that airflow remains outside the setpoint after technician adjustment
rows.plain: Make sure the air movement returns to the required target after a technician changes it during operation. Incorrect airflow could affect the process or cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow remaining off-setpoint after adjustment means the flow rate stays wrong, directly skewing collected volume; severity is moderate because the technician is present and can escalate or abort.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-regulating pumps hold the setpoint reliably after adjustment, but manual units can drift back out, leaving residual off-setpoint outcomes. Adjustment-and-recheck loops are quick on digital units but can drag on manual rotameter samplers. Getting flow to hold at set point after adjustment still leans on technician technique and interpretation on manually adjusted samplers. Repeated adjustment consumes labor time but little material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring glove contamination to the sampling medium during loading or retrieval
rows.plain: Prevent dirty gloves from touching the test material while it is being put in or taken out, so the sample remains clean and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring glove contamination to the medium during loading/retrieval is a handling error tangential to the sampler's automated collection but affecting sample integrity (moderate relevance); it produces false positives forcing investigations and possible batch holds (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Training and glove disinfection reduce transfer risk, yet direct manual handling close to exposed agar leaves a persistent opportunity for contamination. Aseptic loading and retrieval add only a short handling step when materials and positions are properly staged. Avoiding glove contact with exposed medium depends on practiced aseptic technique and sustained attention during awkward manipulations. Glove sanitization and sterile accessories are inexpensive, but contamination can invalidate samples and trigger investigations or repeats.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual contamination being released from the air sampler during handling
rows.plain: Prevent leftover contamination from coming out of the air-sampling device while it is being handled and taken out of service. This helps keep people and nearby areas safe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination released during handling is peripheral to the airflow/collection function but poses serious exposure and cross-contamination risks in controlled environments.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed-bag handling limits release most of the time, but internal deposits in ductwork and heads can shed unpredictably. Containment steps during handling are quick once the unit is bagged, adding little delay. Proper handling to avoid dislodging residue depends on technician care and awareness of hidden internal surfaces. Containment consumables are cheap relative to the risk they mitigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the control media are unsuitable for the planned sampling conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the materials used to prepare a clean reference sample are suitable for the planned sampling conditions. This helps ensure that the sample gives a trustworthy comparison.
rows.imp_rat: Using control media unsuitable for the planned conditions weakens QA validity rather than the primary capture itself, giving moderate relevance; the consequence is compromised data validation that can invalidate a whole sampling batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard control-media selection guidance covers common cases, but edge conditions still lead to unsuitable controls being used. Selecting suitable control media is a quick planning step when references are at hand. Judging control-media suitability for the planned conditions requires knowledge of media performance under those conditions. Unsuitable control media waste the control and undermine the whole comparison.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among approved references for the representative sampling location
rows.plain: Make sure the approved instructions agree on where the sample should be taken. This prevents samples from being collected from the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among approved references about the representative location affects sampling design but not the mechanics of collection, giving moderate relevance; severity is substantial because sampling the wrong location can systematically miss meaningful exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control systems reduce obsolete references, yet inconsistent naming, coordinates, and revisions still create meaningful location ambiguity. Operators can lose setup time reconciling sampling maps, protocols, qualification reports, and batch instructions that identify locations differently. Resolving inconsistent approved references often requires experienced quality or environmental-monitoring personnel rather than straightforward execution. Document reconciliation consumes specialist labor and may trigger change controls, but it rarely demands substantial equipment or material spending.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of safety or compliance sign-off being withheld because the air sampler's contamination and decontamination status is unresolved
rows.plain: Make sure the air-sampling device has clear records showing whether it was contaminated and cleaned, so required approval for disposal is not delayed or denied.
rows.imp_rat: Withheld sign-off due to unresolved contamination status is a compliance-gate error tangential to the sampling act itself, but severity is notable given blocked operations and potential exposure liability.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation of decon status is often incomplete or ambiguous, so sign-off is frequently withheld pending clarification. Waiting on unresolved decon status stalls sign-off and holds up the whole disposal timeline. Resolving contamination/decon status needs QA and EHS judgement that can bottleneck on scarce reviewers. Delayed sign-off ties up staging space and staff, adding carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which airflow at startup deviates from the specified setting
rows.plain: Keep the airflow at startup as close as possible to the required setting. This helps make the sample run consistent and protects the product from unwanted changes in airflow.
rows.imp_rat: Startup airflow deviating from the specified setting biases the sampled volume from the outset, central to accuracy; moderate severity since it can often be corrected before or shortly after the run begins.
rows.sat_rat: Mass-flow-controlled samplers settle to the specified set point quickly and repeatably, though initial overshoot or filter back-pressure can cause minor startup deviation. Flow reaches the set point within seconds of startup, adding negligible delay. Electronic flow control largely removes the manual rotameter-adjustment skill formerly needed at startup. Startup deviation, when it occurs, mainly costs a brief re-verification rather than material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of preparing collection media that do not match the sampling plan or area classification
rows.plain: Prepare collection materials that fit both the sampling instructions and the cleanliness requirements of the area. This helps ensure that the samples can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Preparing media that don't match the sampling plan or area classification undermines valid quantification for that zone (solid relevance); mismatched media can invalidate results and force re-sampling or misclassification (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated media, clear sampling plans, labeling, and lot controls make mismatches uncommon where standard procedures are followed. Approved media specifications, kits, and preplanned staging make correct media preparation fast for routine sampling rounds. Controlled recipes, purchasing specifications, and independent checks substantially reduce reliance on individual judgment. Qualified prepared media and inventory controls have routine costs but minimize waste from unsuitable or expired plates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of air collection components contacting moving fill-line equipment during filling
rows.plain: Keep air-sampling parts from touching equipment that is moving during filling. This helps avoid equipment stoppages and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Contact between sampling components and moving fill-line equipment is not intrinsic to measurement performance, so its relevance is moderate. Its severity is very high because contact can damage equipment, disrupt filling, or contaminate exposed product and critical surfaces.
rows.sat_rat: Physical separation, secured components, and clearance verification make contact uncommon, although loose tubing or setup drift can create residual risk. Predefined clearances and line-clearance checks allow safe setup without materially delaying filling operations. Fixtures and approved routing reduce judgement, though operators must still account for equipment travel and unusual line configurations. Guards and mounting hardware are relatively economical compared with the downtime and damage that contact could cause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between documented and actual collection start and stop points
rows.plain: Make the recorded start and stop points for collecting material match what actually happened. This makes the collection record accurate and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: A discrepancy between documented and actual start or stop points weakens sample-volume, timing, and traceability evidence, giving high relevance; severity is high because data may become uninterpretable or unusable for compliance decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic logs, second-person checks, and batch-record reconciliation make major start-stop discrepancies uncommon, though unsynchronized systems remain a weakness. Instrument timestamps and electronic records capture collection boundaries quickly, although manual transcription and clock reconciliation can add review time. Automated event logs reduce dependence on operator memory, while manual batch records still require careful attention and synchronization. Timestamp functionality is generally included in modern samplers or monitoring systems, and it reduces costly deviation investigations and repeat work.

rows.source_job: Connect power source to air sampler
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of electrical injury or ignition while connecting the air sampler to its power source
rows.plain: The person should avoid electric shock or starting a fire while connecting the air sampler to electricity. The connection needs to be made safely before the sampler is used.
rows.imp_rat: Electrical injury or ignition while connecting power is peripheral to the sampling function itself but carries catastrophic severity involving human harm and fire risk.
rows.sat_rat: Grounded plugs, standardized connectors, and safety-rated equipment make electrical injury or ignition during connection rare in normal practice. Safe connection following standard electrical practice adds negligible time. Basic electrical-safety awareness suffices; no specialized expertise is needed for routine connection. Safety measures are essentially free relative to the injury or ignition costs they prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of usable air sample lost before the alarm condition is resolved
rows.plain: As little usable air sample as possible should be lost before the warning is dealt with. Keeping the sample helps the system detect and respond to the problem accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Usable sample lost before an alarm is resolved reduces yield, moderately relevant to job completeness; severity is moderate since partial samples often remain usable or repeatable.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard means to preserve partial validity, so once an alarm condition begins, sample loss is largely inevitable. The longer the alarm goes unaddressed, the more sample time is lost, and response latency is often long. Minimizing loss depends on rapid, knowledgeable intervention that many operators cannot provide immediately. Air sample already drawn under a fault condition is typically unusable, so the media, effort, and window are wasted with no good salvage practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sampling suitability degrading after repeated cleaning and disinfection exposure
rows.plain: Minimize the chance that repeated cleaning and disinfecting will make the sampling method less suitable for use. This helps the method continue to work reliably over time.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation of sampling suitability after repeated cleaning affects long-term reliability of collection (moderate-high relevance); gradual drift can silently bias counts, a meaningful but less immediately catastrophic severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility claims and maintenance programs address common disinfectants, but repeated exposure to facility-specific agents and sporicides is not always validated over the full service life. Preventive maintenance and periodic calibration detect many degradation effects, but compatibility reviews and replacement cycles add downtime. Routine inspection is straightforward, while identifying subtle material, seal, flow, or electronics degradation requires technical maintenance expertise. Chemical-resistant designs and replaceable components control lifecycle expense, though accelerated aging studies and premature replacements can be costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to contaminants while handling spent filters, cassettes, and consumables
rows.plain: Protect technicians from germs and other harmful material when they handle used filters, holders, and supplies. Safe handling reduces the chance of exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure while handling spent filters and consumables is downstream of the collection job but carries serious health and compliance consequences, especially with hazardous or biological analytes.
rows.sat_rat: Mature PPE and SOP practice keeps exposure incidents uncommon, but lapses and unexpected contamination still produce occasional exposures. Donning PPE and following handling steps adds minor time to the disposal task. Standard PPE and hazard-handling training are widely established, though correct use under real conditions still depends on the technician. PPE and consumables are a routine, low recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between blank-run conditions and the planned real-sampling conditions
rows.plain: Make the blank check as similar as possible to the conditions planned for the real sample. This makes the comparison more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between blank-run and real-sampling conditions weakens quality-control comparability, moderately relevant to defensible results; consequences degrade interpretation but rarely destroy the primary sample.
rows.sat_rat: Documented sampling plans reduce mismatch, but manual parameter entry still leaves gaps between blank and real conditions. Matching blank conditions to planned conditions is a quick configuration step. Ensuring blank-run parameters mirror real sampling relies on the technician correctly transcribing the sampling plan. A mismatched blank offers little cost, but its value is lost if it doesn't represent real conditions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a clean sampling component is recontaminated before the next collection
rows.plain: Keep a clean sample-collection part from becoming contaminated before it is used again. This helps prevent contamination from being carried into the next sample.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination of a clean sampling component before next collection affects sample-integrity readiness rather than the collection act itself (moderate relevance); it can cause false positives and re-sampling but is usually detectable (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning, wrapping, and segregated storage usually maintain cleanliness, with occasional recontamination from damaged packaging or poor handling. Protective wrapping, covered storage, and defined clean-hold times allow components to remain ready without repeated cleaning immediately before use. Labeling and storage procedures are straightforward, although correct aseptic retrieval still requires operator discipline. Sterile packaging and controlled storage consume modest materials and space while reducing reprocessing and rejected components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing the collected sample into the wrong processing workflow
rows.plain: Make sure each collected sample is sent into the correct testing process. This helps ensure the sample receives the right handling and evaluation.
rows.imp_rat: Routing a sample into the wrong processing workflow is moderately relevant since it is a handling/logistics error rather than the core capture step, but consequences can be severe if a critical-area sample is misprocessed and batch disposition relies on it.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode and laboratory-system routing make incorrect workflows rare in mature facilities, with residual errors arising from manual overrides or poorly configured master data. Electronic work queues, destination labels, and scheduled pickups route samples quickly without extensive manual coordination. Automated workflow rules reduce the need to remember laboratory destinations and test requirements, though exceptions still need knowledgeable review. Laboratory information systems require investment but avoid wasted testing, delayed results, and repeat sampling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sample identity becoming disconnected from its collection record during transfer
rows.plain: Keep the sample clearly linked to the record of when and where it was collected while it is being moved. This prevents the sample from being confused with another one.
rows.imp_rat: Losing the link between sample identity and its collection record is a traceability/ambiguity error, moderately relevant to the capture step but severe because unattributable results can invalidate the data and force re-monitoring or batch holds.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant identifiers and electronic custody records make identity disconnection rare, although damaged labels and unrecorded manual transfers remain possible. Durable labels, barcode scans, and electronic chain-of-custody records preserve identity through transfer with minimal added handling time. Machine-readable identifiers and required scan steps reduce reliance on memory and handwriting during handoffs. Labeling and tracking infrastructure costs are modest relative to the batch-impact and investigation costs of an unidentified sample.

rows.source_job: Connect power source to air sampler
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the designated power source is electrically incompatible with the air sampler
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the chosen power supply is unsafe or unsuitable for the air-sampling device. This matters because the device may not work correctly or could be damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Electrical incompatibility of the designated power source is a setup-stage concern less central to sampling accuracy, but severity is high due to potential equipment damage or hazard when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized voltage labeling and universal-input power supplies make gross electrical incompatibility uncommon in practice. Checking voltage/frequency compatibility is a quick pre-connection step. Reading rating labels and matching to the supply requires minimal skill. Incompatibility can damage equipment, but standardized supplies make this cost rare.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that airflow continues after the operator issues the stop command
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that air keeps moving after the operator tells the system to stop. This matters because continued airflow could affect the sampling process.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow continuing after a stop command over-samples and skews the collected volume, undermining accuracy of the job; severity is moderate since the overrun is usually small and can sometimes be accounted for.
rows.sat_rat: Timer- and command-driven shutoff is very deterministic, and residual run-on after a stop command is rare on standard controllers. Stop commands act immediately, adding no meaningful delay to the workflow. Stopping requires essentially no skill; a single command or timer expiry halts the pump. Overrun rarely wastes material, and any small extra volume drawn costs almost nothing.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, procurement officers, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel responsible for sampler selection.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical technical and operational requirements omitted from the candidate air-sampler evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the review of possible air-testing devices does not leave out important technical or day-to-day operating needs. This helps ensure the chosen device will work properly for the job.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical requirements from candidate evaluation is an upstream procurement error, moderately relevant to the job; consequences surface later as unfit equipment but are correctable before deployment.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates help but are inconsistently applied, so critical requirements are routinely missed with no systematic backstop. Building a complete requirements list is front-loaded work that teams often compress under schedule pressure. Enumerating every critical technical and operational requirement depends heavily on the tacit expertise of the specifier, and omissions are common when experienced staff are stretched. Omitted requirements surface as costly change orders or unusable equipment after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous material being released from a power component during handling or transport
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that a power part containing dangerous material leaks or breaks open while being moved or transported. This helps protect people and the surroundings.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous release from a power component during handling is peripheral to the core sampling job (low relevance), but a chemical/electrical hazard release can cause serious injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard battery-removal and containment practice prevents most releases, though damaged or swollen cells occasionally leak or vent. Inspecting and packaging power components adds some handling time but is not a major delay. Recognizing a damaged or leaking battery requires basic hazmat awareness that most technicians have. Approved battery containers and packaging carry a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criterion definitions and scoring rules for viable air sampling approaches
rows.plain: Make the judging rules for methods that check the air clear and specific. This helps everyone score the options the same way and choose a suitable method fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in criterion definitions and scoring rules mainly affects selection or evaluation of sampling approaches rather than individual collections, so relevance is moderate-low; severity is moderate-high because persistent ambiguity can produce a weak or inconsistent monitoring strategy.
rows.sat_rat: No widely accepted scoring framework consistently converts sampler characteristics into facility-specific suitability, leaving evaluations vulnerable to subjective definitions and ratings. Procurement teams can use standards, vendor documentation, and structured scorecards, but translating them into comparable criteria and scoring rules remains time-consuming. Building defensible definitions and weights requires specialized knowledge of microbiology, airflow, regulation, and sampling technology rather than routine purchasing skill alone. Generic qualification templates and consultant support are available, but rigorous comparative testing and expert facilitation can add meaningful evaluation expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required sampling head, stage, or intake component is missing when assembling the sampler body
rows.plain: Make sure all required parts for collecting a sample are available before the sampler is put together. Missing a part could prevent it from working correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A missing sampling head, stage, or intake component during assembly delays or prevents proper configuration, moderately relevant; the consequence is a stopped setup that is usually caught and remediated before sampling.
rows.sat_rat: Packing checklists and kitted sampler cases make complete assembly the norm, though missing small components still occasionally halts setup. A missing component discovered during assembly forces a delay to fetch or source it. Component inventory checks require familiarity with the sampler kit but little specialized skill. Missing parts mainly cost lost time rather than material.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, industrial hygienists, environmental scientists, and field sampling personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of collected sample lost while airflow is terminated
rows.plain: Lose as little of the collected sample as possible when the airflow is stopped. This helps preserve the sample for accurate use or testing.
rows.imp_rat: Sample lost while terminating airflow reduces the captured material central to the collection job (moderately high relevance), but the loss is usually partial and quantifiable rather than result-invalidating (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clean deterministic shutoff means very little sample is lost when airflow is terminated on standard units. Termination is instantaneous so no time is lost in the shutoff itself. Little skill is needed to stop cleanly on a controlled pump. Modern shutoff loses negligible collected sample at termination, so material waste at stop is minimal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous stored energy being released while disconnecting the air sampler
rows.plain: Prevent trapped electrical, air, or other stored power from being released while the air-sampling device is disconnected. This helps protect the person taking it apart.
rows.imp_rat: Stored-energy release while disconnecting is a maintenance-side hazard peripheral to air collection (low relevance), but an energetic discharge can seriously injure the technician (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Widely deployed LOTO practice makes uncontrolled stored-energy release rare when procedures are followed. Lockout/tagout and de-energizing steps add short, predictable time to disconnection. LOTO is a well-trained, codified competency across facilities, lowering reliance on rare expertise. LOTO hardware and procedures are inexpensive and already in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from sharp edges or retained energy during disassembly
rows.plain: Prevent a technician from being cut by sharp edges or hurt by trapped force while taking apart the sampling device. This helps make its reusable parts safer to handle.
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from sharp edges or retained energy during disassembly is a maintenance-safety concern loosely tied to the core sampling job, yet severity is high given irreversible personal injury.
rows.sat_rat: PPE and disassembly SOPs keep injuries uncommon, though unexpected sharp edges and hidden energy still cause occasional incidents. Careful disassembly adds modest time versus rushing. Avoiding sharp-edge and retained-energy injury relies on general mechanical hazard awareness and care during teardown. Cut-resistant gloves and basic tools are inexpensive standard equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in identifying whether the alarm indicates a flow fault, power problem, blockage, or capacity condition
rows.plain: Make each warning clear enough to show whether the problem is poor flow, loss of power, a blockage, or too much material for the equipment to handle. This helps the worker choose the right response.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in distinguishing alarm cause (item 7) affects timely correct response to faults with moderate severity if the wrong remedy is applied; held at the 5.0 mean ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many samplers give only a single generic alarm, so ambiguity about the underlying cause is a frequent, unresolved issue. Diagnosing the alarm type is quick when clearly coded but slow when the indicator is generic. Distinguishing flow, power, blockage, or capacity causes leans heavily on operator troubleshooting expertise. Misdiagnosis leads to wrong corrective action and wasted samples, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between airborne microbial monitoring requirements and contamination risk in each classified zone
rows.plain: Make sure the air-monitoring checks match the risk of contamination in each controlled area. This helps ensure each area is checked often and carefully enough for its risk.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between monitoring requirements and zone contamination risk is a program-design gap affecting where/how much to sample rather than the sampler's direct collection act (moderate relevance); poor requirement mapping can leave high-risk zones under-monitored (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern contamination-control strategies routinely align frequency, location, and method with zone risk, but subjective assumptions and incomplete process knowledge leave recurring gaps. Risk-based monitoring plans and predefined templates accelerate alignment, although updates after facility or process changes still require coordinated review. Qualified teams can apply established risk-assessment methods, but accurate zoning decisions continue to depend on microbiology, process, and airflow expertise. Risk-based tailoring generally directs resources efficiently, although enhanced monitoring in critical zones increases equipment, labor, and consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of programmed sampling cycles that fail to start as scheduled
rows.plain: Make sure scheduled sample-taking cycles start when they are supposed to. This helps prevent needed samples from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Scheduled cycles failing to start means missed monitoring points (moderate relevance), but a missed cycle can usually be rescheduled and creates a data gap rather than corrupted data (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Programmable timers start scheduled cycles reliably, but battery, clock-drift and configuration errors still cause occasional missed scheduled starts. Scheduled starts run unattended, saving the operator from being present at each start time. Programming schedules needs modest setup skill but no ongoing attention. A cycle that fails to start as scheduled wastes the planned sampling window and mandates rescheduling labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual electrical energy in a power component injures the technician during removal
rows.plain: Make sure leftover electricity in a power unit cannot shock or injure the technician while it is being removed. This protects the person handling the unit.
rows.imp_rat: Residual electrical energy injuring a technician during removal is a safety concern tangential to the sampling job (low relevance), yet electric shock can cause severe personal harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established discharge-and-verify practice makes residual-electrical injury uncommon, though it depends on the check actually being performed. Verifying discharge before removal adds only brief time. Capacitor/residual-energy checks are standard electrical safety training with clear procedures. Discharge tools and meters are cheap and commonly available.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of air samplers with run settings that conflict with the coordinated sampling plan
rows.plain: Make sure all air samplers use settings that match the shared sampling plan. This helps the samplers collect the intended information at the right times.
rows.imp_rat: Run settings conflicting with the coordinated plan (item 4) is fairly central to executing the sampling plan but consequences are recoverable by re-running; average kept at or below 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual per-sampler configuration without central verification leaves frequent conflicts with the sampling plan. Programming each sampler to the plan is fast per unit but slow to verify across a fleet. Ensuring settings match the coordinated plan depends on the operator correctly interpreting and entering the plan. Non-conforming settings can invalidate a coordinated campaign, wasting the whole effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sampler connections snagging personnel, carts, or fill-line equipment during filling
rows.plain: Keep sampling tubes and connectors from catching on workers, carts, or filling equipment, because this could interrupt the filling work or cause an accident.
rows.imp_rat: Connections snagging personnel or fill-line equipment is largely a safety/operational nuisance peripheral to quantifying particles (lower relevance), though a fill-line disruption or intervention in a critical zone carries real severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard tie-downs and procedural checks reduce snagging, but exposed connections near crowded fill lines remain a recurring vulnerability. Operators can route and secure tubing and power leads before filling, but line changes and traffic often make setup slow and require mid-run attention. Safe routing depends heavily on operator judgment, familiarity with traffic patterns, and disciplined cable-management technique. Clips, covers, and brackets are inexpensive, although custom routing hardware or remote sampling arrangements can add meaningful expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of interruption causes misidentified from the air sampler's displayed status information
rows.plain: Make sure the displayed status helps correctly identify why air movement stopped. This prevents workers from troubleshooting the wrong problem.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentified interruption causes (item 2) can send operators down the wrong corrective path, moderately central to trustworthy sampling and with real consequences if a run's validity is misjudged, though the physical collection job still occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Many samplers show generic or ambiguous fault indications that operators regularly misread, so this remains a real recurring failure mode. Cause codes appear immediately on the display so no waiting is involved when they are present. Correctly matching a terse fault code to its true cause leans heavily on operator experience and manuals, which is a frequent misjudgement point. Misidentified causes drive unnecessary re-sampling and troubleshooting labor without any purchase reducing the problem.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, field sampling personnel, and equipment custodians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination introduced into the air sampler and sampling pathway during battery access
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material from getting into the air-measuring device or its air path while changing its battery. This helps keep air samples trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during battery access (item 14) can corrupt the sampling pathway and invalidate results, giving moderate-high severity; relevance moderate as battery access is occasional, capped at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Battery compartments near the sampling path make incidental contamination a recurring risk that current practice only partly controls. Clean-handling precautions during battery access add modest time to the task. Preventing contamination of the sampling pathway during battery access demands disciplined technique that varies by technician. Contamination control materials are cheap, but a contaminated pathway can invalidate costly sampling runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transient airborne contamination events go undetected between scheduled samples
rows.plain: Use a way of checking that can detect brief airborne contamination events even when they happen between planned checks.
rows.imp_rat: Transient contamination events missed between scheduled samples reduce monitoring coverage and are moderately relevant to characterizing viable airborne particles. Severity is moderate because a short-lived excursion could remain unknown, though continuous event detection is not always the sampler's intended task.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent active-air samples observe only brief windows, so short contamination events between scheduled collections can routinely escape detection. Scheduled active sampling is operationally convenient, but closing temporal gaps requires longer, more frequent, or near-continuous collection that burdens operations. Designing schedules around interventions and interpreting short-lived signals requires experienced process knowledge and sustained attention. Higher-frequency sampling, continuous microbial monitoring, extra incubation capacity, and data review materially increase equipment and operating costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of obstruction to personnel or material movement caused by air collection components at the fill line
rows.plain: Keep air-collection parts from blocking people or materials moving around the filling line. This helps workers do their jobs without interruptions or unsafe obstacles.
rows.imp_rat: Obstruction of personnel or material movement is secondary to the sampler's collection and quantification job. However, it has moderate severity because interference at the fill line can cause handling errors, operational interruption, or equipment contact.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance reviews and predefined routes partly control obstruction, yet temporary components continue to conflict with personnel and material movement. Planned setup reduces interference, but staff may still slow movements or pause to navigate around sampler heads, stands, cables, and tubing. Safe routing depends on operator awareness and familiarity with line traffic, particularly where access space is limited. Low-profile mounts and routing hardware are affordable, but custom integration or facility modifications can be costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criteria used to classify active-air monitoring results
rows.plain: Make the rules for judging air-sample results as clear as possible. This helps people reach the same decision about whether the air is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in result-classification criteria affects interpretation rather than the physical collection/quantification (moderate relevance); ambiguity can cause inconsistent excursion calls but is correctable through review (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory guidance and site procedures provide a baseline, but local definitions and subjective criteria still produce inconsistent classifications. Approved limits and decision trees accelerate classification, but atypical results often require lengthy interpretation and escalation. Standard procedures reduce routine judgment, yet ambiguous alert, action, excursion, and investigation categories still depend heavily on experienced quality personnel. Existing document controls are inexpensive to operate, although disputed classifications consume investigation and review resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the programmed schedule remains inactive when unattended sampling is due to begin
rows.plain: Make sure the programmed schedule starts the sampling run when it is supposed to, even when no one is present. This helps collect the planned samples on time.
rows.imp_rat: An inactive programmed schedule (item 2) is fairly central for unattended sampling and moderately severe as a missed sampling window loses data; kept at or below 5.0 mean per ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Timer/scheduling features are common but confirming the schedule armed and will trigger is often unverified, so silent no-starts still occur. Programming and arming a schedule takes little time. Confirming that a programmed schedule is actually active demands operator attentiveness to the device state. An inactive schedule means a missed unattended window and a wasted mobilization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shutdown limits allow the sampler to continue operating under unsafe conditions
rows.plain: Set the shutdown limits so the sampler cannot keep running when conditions become unsafe. This helps stop people, equipment, or materials from being put at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Shutdown limits that let the sampler run under unsafe conditions carry real safety stakes and are central to safe operation, held at the ceiling for this lower-ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Better instruments ship with configurable interlocks, yet verifying that limits actually protect against every unsafe condition remains inconsistent and often untested before deployment. Programming shutdown limits is a fast bench step, so duration is not the primary concern here. Knowing what constitutes an unsafe operating condition and encoding it as a shutdown limit demands specialist safety judgement not held by every operator. A shutdown limit that fails to trip risks damaged equipment or an unusable sample, both costly, but the configuration itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between configured alarm thresholds and deployment safety requirements
rows.plain: Make sure alarm limits match the safety needs of the installation, so warnings happen at the right times.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between alarm thresholds and safety requirements can let unsafe conditions go unflagged, which is relevant and consequential, but it is capped at the batch ceiling of 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard SOPs and pre-deployment checklists catch many mismatches, but manual entry against evolving site requirements still leaves recurring silent errors that only surface in the field. Setting alarm thresholds against a deployment safety spec is a quick configuration step once the requirement is known, so time is rarely the binding constraint. Correctly translating site safety requirements into configured thresholds relies on a trained industrial hygienist or safety officer, and that judgement is where mismatches originate. The settings themselves cost nothing, but a mismatch can force an aborted run and re-sampling, which carries real resource waste.

rows.source_job: Connect power source to air sampler
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of battery depletion before the planned sampling run ends
rows.plain: Make sure the battery lasts until the planned air-sampling run is finished.
rows.imp_rat: Battery depletion before the run ends (relevance 5) truncates the collection and defeats the sampling job; severity (5) is high since a partial or aborted run may require a full re-sample.
rows.sat_rat: Fully charged packs, runtime specs, and low-battery alarms make depletion largely avoidable, though long unattended runs still occasionally outlast capacity. Charging or fitting fresh batteries before a run is a routine, low-time task. Estimating whether battery capacity covers the planned run needs only basic runtime knowledge. Premature depletion wastes the run; spare batteries and chargers are cheap insurance.

rows.stk: Laboratory technicians, field sampling personnel, and equipment custodians.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of returning a battery below the specified capacity threshold to service
rows.plain: Do not put the sampler back into use if its battery holds less charge than the required minimum. This helps prevent the sampler from failing during use.
rows.imp_rat: Returning a battery below capacity threshold (item 1) is peripheral to the core sampling job but moderately severe since a dying battery mid-run can abort unattended collection; capped at 5.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Without routine capacity verification, degraded batteries below threshold are returned to service more often than desired. Battery capacity testing takes measurable time and is often skipped under schedule pressure. Interpreting capacity test results requires some expertise and dedicated test equipment not always on hand. Load testers and battery analyzers are affordable, and avoiding premature disposal saves money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminated collection media being routed to an unapproved waste stream
rows.plain: Make sure used materials that may be contaminated are sent to the approved disposal route. This prevents harmful material from entering the wrong waste system.
rows.imp_rat: Routing contaminated media to an unapproved waste stream is a downstream disposal/compliance issue not central to collecting air (low relevance), though improper hazardous waste handling risks regulatory penalties and exposure (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Waste SOPs and labeled bins help, but mislabeling and ambiguous classification still route contaminated media incorrectly at times. Correct waste-stream routing adds documentation and staging time before pickup. Correctly matching contaminated media to the right regulated stream needs waste-classification knowledge that is not evenly distributed. Regulated waste disposal is costly, and misrouting to the wrong stream wastes or risks penalties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging the loaded collection medium while removing it from the sampler
rows.plain: Remove the collected sample without damaging the material holding it. Keeping it intact helps preserve the sample for examination.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging the loaded medium during removal (relevance 5) risks losing an already-collected sample; severity (5) reflects that the collected air data is often irreplaceable once compromised.
rows.sat_rat: Standard handling tools and protocols make intact recovery usual, but manual removal still risks occasional damage to laden media. Removal is a brief step at the close of the run. Extracting a loaded medium intact without dislodging deposit requires careful technique, again skill-dependent. Damaging a loaded sample wastes the entire run's collected material, the costliest loss point.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time airflow remains outside the setpoint before correction
rows.plain: Correct airflow as quickly as possible when it moves away from the target level. This helps keep the process within safe and useful conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Time airflow stays off-setpoint before correction (item 8) affects sample validity and is moderately severe as prolonged off-spec flow biases the sample; kept at 5.0 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Flow controllers and readouts make correction fairly consistent, but the excursion window before an operator acts still varies. Once detected, airflow is usually corrected within minutes, though detection lag extends the excursion. Bringing flow back to setpoint requires modest skill with the sampler controls. Time outside setpoint erodes sample validity, adding potential re-run cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced during mid-run airflow correction
rows.plain: Prevent contamination when the airflow is changed during a run. The adjustment should not allow unwanted material into the process.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during mid-run correction (item 10) can invalidate the sample entirely, giving higher severity, though it is a narrower occurrence; average held at or below 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established clean-handling practice keeps mid-run contamination fairly rare, so this need is reasonably well served. Contamination-safe correction takes a little longer but is not a major time burden. Avoiding contamination during adjustment relies on careful technique and clean handling by the operator. A contaminated sample is wasted, but such events are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the air sampler's status information is unavailable when the sampling run stops
rows.plain: Make sure information about the air sampler’s condition is still available when a sampling run stops unexpectedly. This helps explain what went wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable status information when a run stops (item 5) undermines confidence that the sample is valid, fairly central to a trustworthy sampler and with meaningful consequences if an invalid run goes undetected, though held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Most current samplers retain status through a stop via battery-backed memory, so unavailability is now relatively uncommon though not universal. When information is retained it is available immediately upon stop, with no retrieval delay. Accessing retained status after a stop needs only routine operator competence. Losing status on stop can trigger costly re-runs, but non-volatile logging is now standard and inexpensive.

rows.source_job: Verify sampler operation at run start
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sampler operation is verified against an incorrect airflow setpoint
rows.plain: Make sure the sampler is checked using the correct target airflow. Using the wrong target could make the check unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Verifying against an incorrect airflow setpoint (item 15) undermines confidence in every sample collected, with moderate relevance and severity as it silently biases data; held at the 5.0 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration and setpoint documentation practices catch most errors, but occasional verification against a wrong target still occurs. Confirming the correct setpoint is quick when reference values are at hand. Verifying against the right setpoint depends on operator care in reading the plan. Verifying against a wrong setpoint can invalidate a run, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of clock offset between air samplers before coordinated collection
rows.plain: Make the air-sampling devices show the same time before collecting samples together, so their results can be compared correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Clock offset before coordinated collection (item 5) matters for time-aligned data but its consequences are limited to timestamp reconciliation; capped under 5.0.
