rows.sat_rat: Termination gets done but timing varies, so the gap persists inconsistently. There is often a lag between decommissioning and service termination because the trigger is manual. Coordinating termination across vendors requires contract-management familiarity. The lag directly translates into avoidable service charges.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of canceling services assigned to an air sampler that remains in use
rows.plain: Do not cancel services for an air sampler that is still being used. Canceling them too soon could interrupt needed testing or support.
rows.imp_rat: Canceling services for a still-in-use sampler is an administrative lifecycle error that is peripheral to the core sampling job (relevance low); its consequence is a recoverable service interruption (severity low), and the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset-to-service mapping is often loosely tracked, so cancellation of the wrong unit's service is a real occasional error. Verifying a service belongs to the right unit before canceling adds a quick lookup step. Matching service contracts to the correct asset requires attention but little specialized expertise. Canceling a service for an in-use sampler causes operational disruption and re-instatement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of associated air sampler services left active because cancellation authority is unclear
rows.plain: Make sure unwanted air-sampling services are canceled instead of remaining active because no one knows who can approve the cancellation. This helps prevent unnecessary ongoing services.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving sampler services active due to unclear cancellation authority is a governance ambiguity only tangential to the sampling job (relevance low); the leftover active service is a minor cleanup issue (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unclear ownership of cancellation authority routinely leaves orphaned active services, a common uncontrolled outcome. Resolving who has authority to cancel a service can stall the process and leave services running. Determining cancellation authority is an organizational-knowledge problem more than a technical skill. Services left active bleed subscription and maintenance fees for retired equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required service and monitoring records lost before decommissioning is completed
rows.plain: Make sure no required service or monitoring records are lost before the air-sampling device is taken out of use. These records provide its history and support a complete shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Lost service records before decommissioning is an end-of-life recordkeeping defect, peripheral and low-severity, held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Decommissioning frequently proceeds before records are secured, so record loss remains an inconsistent, procedure-dependent outcome. Capturing service and monitoring records before decommissioning must happen before systems are shut down, a timing constraint often overlooked. Knowing which service logs must be preserved requires operational familiarity with the unit's history. Lost service history forces reconstruction or acceptance of compliance gaps at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stored sampling and calibration records omitted from the export
rows.plain: Make sure the exported records include nearly all stored sampling and calibration information. This preserves the history of checks and measurements when the data is moved.
rows.imp_rat: Records omitted from export is a completeness defect in recordkeeping, peripheral and low-severity here, so both proxies stay at the 1 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Bulk export tools capture most data but silently omit some record types, and completeness is rarely verified. Confirming that all records were exported adds reconciliation time that is frequently skipped. Knowing the full expected record set to check completeness against requires system familiarity. Missing records may require re-access or reconstruction cost after the source is gone.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that exported sampling and calibration records are corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Make sure the transferred records about sampling and equipment checks are complete and can be opened.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted exported records affect recordkeeping rather than the core sampling job, and this batch's ranking caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Common export formats are generally stable, but corruption still slips through when exports go unverified. Validating exported files for readability adds a verification step often omitted under time pressure. Detecting a corrupt or unreadable export requires someone to open and check files in the right software. Corrupt exports force re-export or data loss, wasting effort already spent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported records lacking source-unit, timestamp, or calibration identifiers
rows.plain: Make sure exported sampling records include the source unit, the time they were recorded, and the equipment-check information. This allows people to know where the data came from and whether the measurements can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Exported records lacking identifiers is a traceability ambiguity of modest centrality, capped at 1 by the batch-5 constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Structured exports usually carry timestamps and unit IDs, so identifier loss, while it happens, is less frequent than raw omission. Checking each exported record for its identifiers is tedious and usually spot-checked at best. Recognizing which identifier fields must accompany a record needs data-literacy about calibration provenance. Records lacking source or timestamp identifiers lose usefulness and may require costly re-verification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing access to stored records before the export is completed
rows.plain: Keep the stored records available until copying them out is finished. This prevents missing data if access is interrupted during the export.
rows.imp_rat: Losing access to stored records before export completes is a data-handling annoyance largely tangential to the sampling job, and the batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Access cutoffs are not always coordinated with export completion, so premature loss occurs inconsistently. The core risk is a timing race — records must be exported before access is revoked, and windows are often short and unwatched. Executing a timely export needs familiarity with the data platform's export tools. A missed export window forces expensive data recovery or acceptance of permanent loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled decommissioning delay caused by the data export
rows.plain: Finish copying stored sampling information sooner so it does not delay the planned shutdown. This helps the shutdown remain on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning delay from data export is a wind-down scheduling issue peripheral to active sampling with low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Exports usually complete but occasionally hit format or access snags that extend the delay. Data export before decommissioning can hold up the schedule when volumes are large or formats are legacy. Extracting data from proprietary sampler systems can need vendor or IT expertise. Export effort is mostly labor cost, generally contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that administrator access restrictions block clearance before the air sampler is transferred or disposed of
rows.plain: Make sure stored settings and information can be cleared before the air sampler is handed over or thrown away. This prevents access rules from stopping the cleanup.
rows.imp_rat: Access restrictions blocking clearance before transfer/disposal is a lifecycle-delay issue peripheral to sampling (relevance low), with a recoverable scheduling consequence (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Whether an admin restriction blocks clearance is somewhat predictable if roles are documented, giving middling consistency. Admin lockouts can freeze clearance for days while the right authority is located. The core problem is dependence on a specific privileged administrator whose access rights are the bottleneck. Delays add labor cost but no major material spend.

rows.source_job: Transfer ownership of the air sampler
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the air sampler transfer is blocked by an unresolved ownership claim or contractual restriction
rows.plain: The transfer of the air sampler should not be stopped by someone else claiming ownership or by an agreement that limits the transfer. This helps ensure the new owner can legally receive it.
rows.imp_rat: A transfer blocked by an ownership claim is a contractual unreliability issue peripheral to the sampling job (relevance low); it only delays transfer (severity low), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Lease, lien, or shared-ownership entanglements on instruments are unpredictable and occasionally block transfers outright, so certainty is only moderate. An unresolved ownership claim can stall a transfer indefinitely while legal review proceeds. Resolving contractual restrictions requires legal expertise not always on hand for routine disposal. Legal escalation and potential contract remediation carry real cost when claims surface.

rows.source_job: Transfer ownership of the air sampler
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required air sampler ownership-transfer documents containing missing, inaccurate, or conflicting information
rows.plain: Make sure every document used to transfer ownership of the air-sampling device is complete and says the same correct information. This helps avoid confusion about who owns the device and its history.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting ownership-transfer documents is a paperwork defect remote from the sampling job (relevance low), with a recoverable consequence (severity low), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership-transfer paperwork routinely carries missing or conflicting fields because source systems disagree, making this a persistent reliability gap. Chasing corrected transfer documents extends the handover timeline noticeably. Detecting conflicting or inaccurate entries across documents needs an attentive reviewer who knows the asset's history. Document rework is cheap in materials but ties up compliance time.

rows.source_job: Transfer ownership of the air sampler
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required approvals and signatures from the air sampler transfer parties
rows.plain: Get all required approvals and signatures quickly when the air sampler changes hands. This helps complete the transfer without leaving ownership uncertain.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on transfer approvals and signatures is a paperwork delay peripheral to the sampling function with minor consequences; ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approvals do eventually arrive under existing sign-off practice, but the timing is unpredictable and stalls are common. Approval and signature routing at disposal is still largely serial email/ticket-based, so units routinely sit waiting days to weeks for transfer sign-offs. Chasing the right approvers requires institutional knowledge of who owns what, and that judgement rests with a few experienced coordinators. The waiting itself burns little material cost but ties up staff time and keeps assets nominally in service longer than needed.

rows.source_job: Transfer ownership of the air sampler
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controlled legal, quality, and asset records that identify the prior owner after the air sampler transfer
rows.plain: Avoid leaving old-owner names on important legal, quality, or equipment records after the air sampler changes hands. This keeps records accurate and shows who is responsible now.
rows.imp_rat: Lingering prior-owner records after transfer is a data-governance defect with low relevance to sampling and limited severity; ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Residual owner identifiers commonly persist in some controlled records after transfer, making complete scrubbing unreliable. Purging prior-owner identifiers from records is a slow, dispersed cleanup task. Knowing every controlled legal, quality, and asset record that references the owner demands broad records expertise. Cleanup is labor-intensive but low in material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual sampler contamination causing a recycling channel to reject components
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leftover material on sampling parts causes a recycling company to reject them. This helps the parts enter the recycling process.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination causing a recycling channel to reject components is an end-of-life disposal error, remote from the sampling job with minor consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination status is inconsistently documented and verified at end-of-life, so recycler rejection is an unpredictable recurring outcome. Decontaminating a sampler enough to satisfy a recycler adds handling time that is rarely budgeted at disposal. Judging whether residual contamination will trigger recycler rejection requires knowledge of both the sampled media and recycler acceptance rules that disposal staff seldom have. Rejected loads must be re-handled or landfilled, wasting the recycling value and incurring rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recoverable components assigned to incompatible recycling streams
rows.plain: Fewer reusable sampling parts should be put into the wrong recycling category. Correct sorting helps ensure each part is handled in the proper way.
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable components assigned to incompatible recycling streams is an end-of-life sorting error, remote from the sampling job with trivial functional consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Established e-waste and metals recycling channels exist but component-level sorting for niche instruments is inconsistently applied. Sorting components into correct recycling streams adds sorting time proportional to how mixed the materials are. Correct stream assignment depends on someone identifying material types and electronics categories, a judgement that varies with recycler literacy. Misassigned recoverables lose their recycling value and may incur contamination penalties, a recurring avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of recoverable material discarded with nonrecoverable waste
rows.plain: Throw away less material that could still be recovered and used, instead of mixing it with material that cannot be reused. This saves useful material and reduces waste.
rows.imp_rat: Discarding recoverable material with waste is a housekeeping/cost concern essentially unrelated to collecting air, with trivial consequences to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Diversion rates vary widely by site and hauler, so the fraction wrongly discarded is inconsistent and largely uncontrolled. Separating recoverable material from waste takes extra sorting time that disposal workflows often skip under pressure. Identifying which parts are recoverable requires some material knowledge, though general recycling awareness partly covers it. Recoverable material tossed with general waste is a direct loss of recovery value and is a common outcome when disposal is rushed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of routing delays caused by incomplete recycling documentation
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for recyclable parts to be sent onward when recycling paperwork is incomplete. Complete information helps the parts move through the recycling process sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Routing delays from incomplete recycling documentation are end-of-life logistics essentially unrelated to collecting air, with low-stakes consequences; both relevance and severity are pinned at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established manifest and chain-of-custody procedures exist and mostly work, so delays are more the exception than the norm. Incomplete recycling paperwork routinely stalls units at the routing/disposition point until documents are completed. Assembling correct recycling and waste manifests for sampler materials requires regulatory familiarity that not all disposal staff possess. Routing delays incur storage and handling costs while units wait for documentation to clear.

rows.source_job: Notify stakeholders of air sampler retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant internal or external stakeholders omitted from the air sampler retirement notification
rows.plain: Tell every important person and group, inside or outside the organization, that the air-sampling device is being retired. This helps prevent anyone from being caught by surprise or relying on equipment that is no longer in use.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from a retirement notification is a communications defect far removed from actually sampling air, with recoverable consequences; both relevance and severity are held at 1 under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Distribution lists are usually ad hoc and rarely maintained, so overlooked stakeholders are a persistent and common failure. Rebuilding a complete stakeholder list each time delays the notification while omissions are discovered late. Knowing every relevant internal and external party for a given sampler relies on tribal knowledge held by a few long-tenured staff. Omissions cost mainly rework and occasional compliance exposure rather than direct spend.

rows.source_job: Notify stakeholders of air sampler retirement
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the air sampler's operational status change and stakeholder notification
rows.plain: Notify the affected people as soon as the air-sampling device’s status changes, such as when it is retired. This keeps them aware of its availability and use.
rows.imp_rat: Notifying stakeholders of an air sampler's operational status change is administrative overhead peripheral to the core job of collecting air; delay here has low relevance and mild consequences, so both proxies are capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual notification means the interval between change and notice is inconsistent and sometimes forgotten entirely. Notifications typically lag the actual status change because they are triggered manually after the fact rather than at the moment of change. Prompt notification depends on someone remembering the distribution steps, a low-skill but attention-heavy task. The delay costs little in resources, mostly the labor of composing and sending updates.

rows.source_job: Notify stakeholders of air sampler retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended stakeholders who do not receive the air sampler retirement notification
rows.plain: Make sure everyone who needs to know receives notice that the device used to collect air samples is being taken out of use. This prevents people from relying on equipment that is no longer available.
rows.imp_rat: Failed delivery of a retirement notification is a peripheral messaging reliability issue with minor, correctable fallout relative to the CFJ; both proxies stay at 1 per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Email and portal notices generally reach their targets, but without read/acknowledgement tracking a fraction silently fail to land. Delivery failures usually only come to light later, adding delay when a stakeholder acts on outdated status. Confirming receipt requires little skill but the vigilance to follow up is often lacking. Non-delivery wastes little material, mostly the cost of remediation when someone missed the notice.

rows.source_job: Notify stakeholders of air sampler retirement
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders receive conflicting information about the air sampler's retirement, transfer, or operational status
rows.plain: Make sure all affected people receive the same accurate information about whether the air sampler is being retired, moved, or still in use.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting stakeholder information about retirement/transfer status is a communication ambiguity peripheral to sampling with limited severity; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single authoritative source of truth for retirement status, mixed messages from separate spreadsheets and emails are a frequent, recurring failure. Conflicting status information tends to surface late, after stakeholders have already acted on stale data. Keeping every channel consistent requires a communications discipline and asset-register knowledge that few coordinators reliably maintain. Reconciling conflicting messages wastes staff effort but little material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the authorization package being returned because the air sampler's identity, ownership, or retirement records are incomplete
rows.plain: Avoid having the disposal request sent back because information about the air sampler’s identity, owner, or retirement is missing. Complete records help show that disposal is authorized.
rows.imp_rat: An authorization package returned for incomplete retirement records is administrative rework tangential to the CFJ (relevance low), with a delay-only consequence (severity low), limited to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset registers for older air samplers are frequently incomplete on identity or ownership, so returns for missing records are a common, recurring failure. Package returns add days-to-weeks of rework loops, but the delay is a symptom rather than the core loss. Assembling a complete identity and retirement record demands familiarity with asset registers that only experienced records staff possess. Rework and resubmission consume administrative labor but rarely large material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the disposal authorization request remains pending with asset owners, safety officers, and compliance teams
rows.plain: Get the disposal request approved promptly by the people responsible for the equipment, safety, and rules.
rows.imp_rat: Time a disposal authorization sits pending across teams is a bureaucratic delay peripheral to the sampling job with low severity; held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approvals do eventually come but dwell time is highly variable. Disposal authorization requests routinely sit pending with owners, safety, and compliance for extended periods. Expediting requires knowing how to navigate multiple sign-off queues. Pending time carries holding and storage cost for the retired unit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among asset owners, safety officers, and compliance teams about the conditions for disposing of the air sampler
rows.plain: Make sure the asset owners, safety officers, and compliance teams agree on when the air-sampling device may be thrown away. This helps ensure disposal is safe and properly approved.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement over disposal conditions is a stakeholder-alignment ambiguity at end of life, distant from core sampling and contained in impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Owners, safety officers, and compliance teams frequently hold conflicting views on disposal conditions, so consensus is inconsistent. Disagreement among stakeholders drags out resolution meetings and extends timelines. Reconciling differing conditions needs negotiation and cross-domain judgement. Disputes cost time more than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory disposal approvals without an identified accountable approver
rows.plain: Make sure every required disposal approval has a clearly named person responsible for giving it. This prevents disposal decisions from being delayed or left unowned.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal approvals lacking an accountable approver is a governance ambiguity in end-of-life processing, peripheral to sampling with modest consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approver assignment is often incomplete for disposal steps, so orphaned approvals recur. Missing an accountable approver stalls the approval chain until one is found. Mapping each mandatory approval to a named approver requires governance knowledge. Gaps cost coordination effort more than money.

rows.stk: New or re-assigned laboratory technicians, field samplers, industrial hygienists, and environmental monitoring personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic handling requirements the operator cannot correctly explain or demonstrate after learning
rows.plain: Make sure the operator can explain and show how to handle samples without introducing contamination after training. This helps the operator perform the procedure safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Operator inability to explain aseptic handling is a training-quality gap of modest centrality, held at the mandated ceiling of 1 for both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Competency assessments exist and catch gross gaps, but retained, correctly-demonstrable aseptic skill still varies between operators. Training on aseptic handling takes scheduled time but is a one-off investment rather than a per-run delay. Aseptic technique is exactly a scarce operator-competency need, and demonstrable mastery varies widely across staff after standard training. Training consumes instructor and media resources but is a routine budgeted cost.

rows.stk: New or re-assigned laboratory technicians, field samplers, industrial hygienists, and environmental monitoring personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sampler setup and operating steps performed out of sequence during supervised practice
rows.plain: Avoid having operators perform important setup and operating steps for the sampler in the wrong order during practice. Following the correct order helps the sampler work properly and produces reliable results.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence setup steps during supervised practice is a training artifact with limited real-consequence, kept at 1 per the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Structured checklists and supervised drills reduce sequence errors, but out-of-order steps still appear during learning and depend on trainer vigilance. Supervised practice sessions take dedicated time but are planned, not an operational delay. Performing setup and operating steps in correct sequence is a core skill-acquisition need, and out-of-sequence errors are the hallmark of under-practiced operators. Practice uses some consumables and supervisor time, a manageable training cost.

rows.stk: New or re-assigned laboratory technicians, field samplers, industrial hygienists, and environmental monitoring personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between the instruction received and the current approved procedure for the sampler model
rows.plain: Make sure the instructions received agree with the currently approved instructions for using the air sampler. This helps the operator use it correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between instruction and approved procedure is a procedural ambiguity of limited centrality here, and the batch-5 cap forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Verbal or outdated instructions still frequently conflict with the current controlled procedure, and there is weak routine enforcement to catch model-specific mismatches. Reconciling a conflicting instruction against the approved procedure costs some time to clarify. Recognizing that an instruction conflicts with the current approved procedure for a specific model demands experienced judgement the operator may lack. Following the wrong instruction can waste a run's materials, but the immediate loss is procedural integrity.

rows.stk: New or re-assigned laboratory technicians, field samplers, industrial hygienists, and environmental monitoring personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the operator waits to access the required sampler model for hands-on learning
rows.plain: Make the correct sampler available quickly so the operator can practice using it directly.
rows.imp_rat: Operator wait time to access a sampler for learning is a scheduling delay only loosely central, held at the mandated 1.
rows.sat_rat: Access generally comes through eventually via scheduling, so the delay is intermittent rather than a hard block. Waiting for a free unit of the required sampler model for hands-on learning can delay training when equipment is scarce. The wait is an equipment-access constraint, though it does gate skill development. Dedicating a working sampler to training ties up an asset but wastes little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of remote-interface unavailability while the sampler is inaccessible
rows.plain: Keep the remote connection to the sampling device available for as much time as possible when people cannot reach it directly. This lets operators continue controlling it from a distance.
rows.imp_rat: Remote-interface unavailability while the sampler is inaccessible is a peripheral connectivity delay that does not stop physical air collection and has minor consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Remote-interface uptime for field air samplers is dependent on power and connectivity that are frequently unreliable, so unavailability recurs during exactly the moments access is needed. When a sampler is physically inaccessible, the remote interface is often also unavailable for extended periods, prolonging blind spots. Operators must plan around interface outages using experience, since little automated failover exists. Interface downtime can force site trips or lost monitoring windows, adding cost.

rows.source_job: Recover calibration standards and reference materials
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reusable standards or reference materials are discarded with the retiring air sampler
rows.plain: Make sure reusable checking tools and comparison materials are saved instead of being thrown away with the old air-checking device.
rows.imp_rat: Discarding reusable standards with a retiring sampler is a waste concern peripheral to collecting air (relevance low), with a modest replacement-cost consequence (severity low), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard end-of-life salvage step for reference materials, so their loss with the unit happens inconsistently. Recovering reusable standards before disposal adds a brief extraction step easily skipped when discarding the unit. Recognizing which reference materials are reusable requires knowledge of their type and remaining validity. Discarding reusable standards with the sampler directly wastes valuable, sometimes expensive, reference materials.

rows.source_job: Recover calibration standards and reference materials
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in matching standards and reference materials to the retiring air sampler
rows.plain: Be more certain that the measuring materials and comparison samples belong with the air-sampling machine being retired. This helps prevent mix-ups when the machine's past measurements are checked.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in matching reference materials to a retiring sampler is a retirement-phase ambiguity peripheral to active sampling (relevance low), with a recoverable sorting consequence (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy air samplers often have fragmented or lost calibration lineage, so the certainty of correctly matching reference materials is genuinely low and inconsistent. Matching calibration standards and reference materials to a retiring air sampler is a one-time lookup that adds little schedule time when records exist. It relies on metrology or QA specialists interpreting incomplete calibration histories, which is scarce judgement-heavy work at disposal. The cost of reconciling standards is modest but can require re-testing when documentation gaps force conservative assumptions.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, industrial hygienists, equipment custodians, and field sampling personnel.
rows.source_job: Transition sampling operations to replacement unit
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of divergence between replacement-unit sampling results and validated sampling expectations under equivalent conditions
rows.plain: Make sure samples from the replacement unit produce results that match the results expected from earlier checks done in the same conditions. This helps show that the replacement unit works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Divergence of replacement-unit results from validated expectations touches data quality but is scored here below the prior batch; capped at 1.0 despite its measurement relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Equivalence protocols and validation acceptance criteria exist and are widely applied, so divergence is usually caught, though borderline cases still slip through. Comparing replacement-unit results against validated expectations adds a verification window before the old unit is retired. Judging whether a replacement sampler reproduces validated performance demands calibration and QA expertise that is scarce outside dedicated metrology teams. Side-by-side or reference comparisons consume calibration standards, technician hours and sometimes duplicate sampling runs.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, industrial hygienists, equipment custodians, and field sampling personnel.
rows.source_job: Transition sampling operations to replacement unit
rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unclear operational responsibility for the replacement air sampler after cutover
rows.plain: Make it clear as soon as possible who is responsible for running the replacement air-measuring device after the changeover. This helps prevent missed work or confusion.
rows.imp_rat: Unclear operational responsibility after cutover is a transitional ownership ambiguity with moderate relevance but held below prior batch, so capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Few organizations have a formalized cutover handover with explicit accountability transfer, so unclear ownership recurs and is a real weak point. Ownership hand-off at cutover is often informal, so an ambiguous responsibility gap can persist for days after switchover. Clean cutover depends on a coordinator who knows both the outgoing and incoming operational owners, which is not always available. Unclear responsibility rarely costs money directly but risks missed monitoring coverage during the gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the certificate content is illegible
rows.plain: Make sure the information on the calibration certificate is easy to read. This helps people confirm that the equipment was properly checked.
rows.imp_rat: Illegible certificate content is a documentation-legibility ambiguity remote from the sampling job (relevance low); it can be reissued (severity low), so it stays at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Most certificates now arrive as clean digital PDFs, so illegibility is uncommon but still occurs with scanned or poorly rendered documents. Illegible certificates force re-requests that delay verification. Reading a certificate needs no special skill unless it is degraded. Requesting a legible replacement carries little cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting versions of the certificate accompany the device
rows.plain: Make sure the device does not come with different versions of its calibration certificate, so people know which information is correct.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting certificate versions accompanying the device is a documentation-version error, administrative and peripheral to sampling, so both proxies are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control practices make conflicting certificate versions relatively rare, though they still appear occasionally with multi-source shipments. Spotting duplicate or conflicting certificate versions at receipt is a fast check. Identifying version conflicts requires basic document-control attention. Conflicting certificates cause minor rework rather than major cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing warranty eligibility because the ASIP air sampler registration deadline is missed
rows.plain: Register the air-sampling device before its registration deadline. This helps preserve the right to make warranty claims.
rows.imp_rat: Missing a registration deadline that voids warranty is a financial/administrative risk peripheral to the sampling job with contained consequences; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deadline tracking relies on ad hoc calendar entries or human memory rather than an enforced system, so misses recur whenever the reminder is never set. Registration deadlines are typically - , giving ample time, so the failure is not caused by insufficient time when someone is tracking it. Meeting a deadline requires no expertise, only that a reminder or calendar entry exists and is monitored. Missing the deadline forfeits warranty eligibility, a significant sunk-cost loss on an expensive instrument, though the tracking itself is nearly free.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of submitted ASIP air sampler registration fields containing incorrect product, purchaser, or installation-site details
rows.plain: Enter the correct product, buyer, and installation-location details when registering the air sampler for warranty service. Correct information helps the warranty be linked to the right product and place.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect product/purchaser/site details in ASIP registration fields is a data-entry defect tangential to sampling (relevance low), correctable by editing (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual data entry into registration systems without cross-validation produces frequent incorrect field values. Correcting incorrect ASIP registration fields requires resubmission cycles. Entering accurate product, purchaser, and site details demands the registrant have the correct source information at hand. Incorrect registrations can affect warranty or compliance status, with potential downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that activated warranty or support coverage differs from the entitlement purchased for the ASIP air sampler
rows.plain: Make sure the air sampler receives the warranty and support that were actually purchased. This prevents gaps in help or coverage when they are needed.
rows.imp_rat: Activated coverage differing from purchased entitlement is a warranty-alignment defect remote from the sampling job (relevance low), with a correctable billing consequence (severity low), held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual cross-checking of quotes, invoices, and vendor portals that rarely reconcile automatically, so entitlement drift goes undetected until it fails at the point of need. Verifying that activated coverage matches the purchased entitlement usually surfaces only when a warranty claim is filed, so delays in detecting a mismatch can stretch across weeks or months. Reconciling a purchase order line-item against the vendor's activated support tier takes procurement diligence but no scarce expertise, though it does depend on someone's careful attention that is often absent. A silent entitlement mismatch means paying for coverage not received, but the dollar exposure is modest relative to the sampler and only realized at claim time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the ASIP air sampler registration submission is not recorded by the responsible party
rows.plain: Make sure the responsible person records the air sampler’s registration submission. This provides proof that the registration was completed.
rows.imp_rat: A registration submission not recorded by the responsible party is a record-keeping unreliability tangential to sampling (relevance low), resolvable by re-submission (severity low), held at the batch ceiling of 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Many vendor registration channels give no closed-loop acknowledgement, so submissions vanish silently and the acquirer has no consistent proof of record. Registration submissions can be sent quickly, but confirmation that the responsible party actually recorded them often lags or never arrives. Submitting a registration form is a low-skill clerical task, so the failure is not caused by lack of expertise. A lost registration costs little to re-submit, but an unrecorded one can forfeit warranty value that dwarfs the effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required serial, purchase, or coverage records are unavailable when registering the ASIP air sampler
rows.plain: Make sure the device's serial number, purchase details, and warranty information are available when its warranty is registered. This helps ensure the right device receives the correct coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable serial/coverage records when registering the ASIP sampler is a records-availability ambiguity tangential to sampling (relevance low), with a correctable lookup delay (severity low), held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a centralized, linked record of serial and coverage data, these records are commonly unavailable when registration is attempted. Missing serial, purchase, or coverage records stall ASIP registration while they are tracked down. Retrieving these records requires knowing the acquisition paper trail rather than deep expertise. Absent coverage records can void warranty claims, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of specification or pricing data becoming outdated before the comparison is completed
rows.plain: Avoid using old product details or prices before the comparison is finished. Current information is needed to choose fairly between the available samplers.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated specification/pricing data during comparison is a procurement-timing instability unrelated to collecting air, with negligible job consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Quotes carry expiration dates but manual comparison spreadsheets often outlast them, so decisions on outdated data occur. Comparisons drag because quotes must be gathered from multiple suppliers, and prices or specs can shift before the analysis finishes. Tracking which spec or price versions are current requires diligence but not deep expertise. Acting on stale pricing can lock in higher costs or miss a better option, wasting budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of procurement approval delays before order submission
rows.plain: Get the required purchasing approval before sending the order. This helps the purchase move forward without waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Procurement approval delays are administrative and tangential to the sampling job, and the ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Digital approval systems make routing consistent, yet absent approvers and escalation gaps still cause unpredictable holds. Procurement approval workflows frequently stall orders while awaiting sign-offs, a recurrent source of delay. Moving an order through approvals needs process familiarity rather than deep expertise. Approval delays rarely add hard cost but can miss quote validity windows.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of purchase-order rejection because the transaction was routed through the wrong purchasing channel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the order is rejected because it was sent through the wrong company purchasing process. This helps ensure the purchase can go through without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Purchase-order rejection from wrong routing is a procurement rework issue unrelated to the sampling function (relevance low); it merely requires resubmission (severity low), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Procurement systems increasingly enforce channel routing rules, so rejections for this reason are declining though not eliminated. A PO rejected for wrong channel must be re-routed and resubmitted, adding delay. Knowing the correct purchasing channel requires familiarity with organizational procurement rules. Wrong-channel routing wastes staff effort more than money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to obtain the supplier’s confirmed delivery commitment for the air sampler order
rows.plain: Find out the supplier’s firm delivery date for the air-measuring device as quickly as possible. This makes it easier to plan the work that depends on it.
rows.imp_rat: Time to obtain a delivery commitment is a procurement-timing concern only loosely tied to the sampling job, capped at 1 by the batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers usually provide commitments but their timeliness and firmness vary, so confirmation reliability is uneven. Obtaining a confirmed delivery commitment can take days of supplier follow-up, though established suppliers often respond quickly. Chasing and interpreting delivery commitments is routine buyer work needing little special skill. Delays in confirmation carry little direct cost beyond staff follow-up time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the air sampler is shipped to an incorrect or incomplete delivery address
rows.plain: The air sampler should be sent to a complete and correct address. This prevents delivery delays, misdelivery, or a lost shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Shipping to a wrong address is a logistics defect peripheral to the sampling function itself; per the ordering it cannot exceed the 1 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Address validation in modern ordering and shipping systems catches most errors, though incomplete site details still occasionally cause misdelivery. A misdelivery causes significant delay while the unit is located and reshipped. Entering a correct delivery address is routine clerical work requiring little specialized skill. Wrong or incomplete addresses generate redelivery fees and possible loss, but these are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved shipping-term responsibility conflicts when the air sampler order is released
rows.plain: Settle any disagreement about who is responsible for shipping the air-sampling device when the order is released. This helps prevent delays and unexpected costs.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved shipping-term responsibility conflicts are a contractual ambiguity unrelated to sampling performance, with trivial impact on the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO terms and supplier agreements usually clarify responsibility, but ambiguities still slip through on nonstandard shipments. Resolving a term-responsibility conflict at order release adds negotiation delay before shipment can proceed. Interpreting Incoterms and matching them to insurance and liability responsibilities needs contract expertise not always on hand. Unresolved responsibility gaps can leave the buyer paying for loss or damage that should have been the supplier's.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unplanned freight, duty, tax, or handling charges caused by the agreed shipping terms
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected shipping, import, tax, or handling costs caused by how delivery responsibilities were assigned. This helps reveal the true cost of completing the order.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned freight/duty/handling charges from shipping terms are a purely commercial cost error unrelated to the sampling function, with trivial job consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Charge surprises are common and unpredictable since the true landed cost is rarely computed before the order is placed. Surprise charges typically appear at delivery or invoicing, causing delays only when disputes arise. Correctly applying Incoterms to anticipate duty, tax, and handling requires trade knowledge that general buyers rarely possess. Unplanned freight, duty, and handling charges are a frequent budget overrun because shipping terms are often accepted without full cost modeling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time until required network and security authorization is completed for the air sampler
rows.plain: The air sampler should receive the required permission to connect to the organization’s computer network as soon as possible. This allows its connected services to be turned on without unnecessary waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Time to complete network/security authorization is an administrative onboarding delay with low relevance to actual sampling and modest consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-management and accreditation workflows make the eventual outcome fairly certain even if slow, so the dominant pain is delay rather than failure. Network and security authorization for a networked sampler routinely bottlenecks in IT/security queues and can take days to weeks depending on organizational backlog. Completing the authorization requires coordinating with security staff and understanding accreditation requirements, demanding cross-functional expertise that is often scarce. The authorization process consumes staff labor hours but little direct material cost, so overspend is not the primary loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the air sampler is associated with the wrong site or service account during activation
rows.plain: The air sampler should be linked to the correct location and service account when it is turned on. This prevents its information and support from being assigned to the wrong place or organization.
rows.imp_rat: Associating the sampler with the wrong site or account during activation is an administrative mapping error (relevance low) whose misrouting is correctable (severity low), and the ranking limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual selection from account lists during activation is error-prone with limited validation, so wrong associations happen with some regularity. Correcting a wrong site or account association after activation requires rework time. Selecting the correct site or service account requires familiarity with the account hierarchy that non-specialists may lack. Mis-association can misroute data or billing but is usually correctable at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the administrator lacks required credentials or permissions when activating connected services
rows.plain: The administrator should have the required login details and permissions before turning on connected services. This prevents the setup from being delayed or blocked.
rows.imp_rat: An administrator lacking credentials when activating connected services is a peripheral onboarding delay (relevance low) with an easily-absorbed consequence of a stalled activation (severity low), held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Permission requirements are inconsistently documented across vendors, so administrators frequently arrive at activation under-provisioned. Missing admin credentials at activation stall the process while permissions are obtained. The need centers on the administrator having the right permissions and know-how, which is often unclear until activation is attempted. Credential gaps mostly cost time rather than money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the activated service cannot establish a connection with the air sampler
rows.plain: Make sure the connected service can communicate with the air sampler after it is turned on.
rows.imp_rat: The service failing to connect to the air sampler is peripheral to the core sampling job and its consequences are minor since manual workarounds exist; per the batch-5 ceiling it stays capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Connectivity between activated service and device depends on many uncontrolled factors and is not reliably validated before acquisition closes. A service that cannot connect triggers troubleshooting cycles that delay commissioning of the sampler. Diagnosing why an activated service fails to connect requires networking and device-configuration skill beyond typical procurement staff. Failed connections generate support calls and possible site revisits that add cost.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, procurement officers, receiving staff, and field equipment coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sampler documents absent from the shipment
rows.plain: Make sure every required document for the sampling device is included in the shipment. This helps staff confirm that the correct order was received.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required documents from the shipment is a paperwork-completeness error, administrative and recoverable, so both proxies are held at the low end.
rows.sat_rat: Receiving checklists specify required documents, but document sets are more variable than hardware, so absences recur somewhat more often. Verifying document completeness at receipt is a modest, routine task. Knowing which documents are mandatory for a sterile-facility device requires some QA familiarity but is well established. Missing documents delay qualification but are recoverable at low direct cost.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, procurement officers, receiving staff, and field equipment coordinators.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency across the purchase order, packing records, and shipment labels
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase order, package records, and shipping labels all describe the same order. This helps confirm that the sampler shipment is correct.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency across PO, packing, and labels is a logistics-documentation ambiguity of low centrality, capped at 1 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documents are generated by different parties and systems that are seldom fully integrated, so inconsistencies across them are common. Reconciling PO, packing records, and labels adds handling time only when inconsistencies are noticed. Cross-checking these documents is clerical and needs modest skill. Inconsistencies cause receiving disputes and rework that consume resources.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, equipment custodians, and quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Register sampler and record acquisition metadata
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sampler registrations with required acquisition or identification metadata missing
rows.plain: Make sure each sampler’s record includes all required details about what was collected, when, and how it is identified. This matters so the collected information can be understood and linked to the right sample.
rows.imp_rat: Missing registration acquisition/identification metadata is a data-completeness defect peripheral to sampling (relevance low); it is correctable on resubmission (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Registration forms often allow submission with blank fields and lack strong validation, so missing required metadata is a recurring problem. Chasing down missing registration metadata after submission adds delay. Knowing which acquisition and identification fields are required takes registration-process familiarity. Incomplete registrations mainly cost rework time, not money.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, equipment custodians, and quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Register sampler and record acquisition metadata
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sampler records containing metadata that does not match the received unit or its acquisition documents
rows.plain: Make sure the information recorded about each sampler matches the actual item received and its paperwork. This prevents samples from being mixed up or traced incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Metadata mismatch on sampler records is a documentation defect of limited stakes for the sampling job, capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Metadata is often keyed manually from documents without automated cross-check against the delivered unit, so mismatches recur. Reconciling mismatched metadata against the received unit and documents is a slow manual check when discrepancies appear. Verifying that record metadata matches the physical unit requires attention to detail and product familiarity. Mismatched records cause downstream asset-management errors that are costly to unwind.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, equipment custodians, and quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Register sampler and record acquisition metadata
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning the acquired sampler an identifier already associated with another asset
rows.plain: Give the newly acquired sampler a new identifier that is not already assigned to another piece of equipment. Duplicate identifiers can cause records, maintenance, or test results to be linked to the wrong asset.
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a duplicate asset identifier is an inventory-management ambiguity of limited centrality here, kept at the 1 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Asset-management systems that auto-generate or check IDs largely prevent duplicates, though manual assignment still occasionally collides. Detecting and resolving a duplicate identifier after the fact costs investigation time. Assigning a unique identifier is straightforward when a registry tool is used. Duplicate IDs rarely cost money directly but can corrupt asset tracking.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, equipment custodians, and quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Register sampler and record acquisition metadata
rows.stmt: Minimize the time from sampler receipt until its registration is complete
rows.plain: Finish recording the sampler in the organization’s records as soon as it is received. This makes the device ready to track and links it to the information about how it was obtained.
rows.imp_rat: Time from receipt to registration is a logistical bookkeeping step with peripheral relevance and trivial severity for the sampling job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because there is rarely a triggered workflow tying receipt to registration, the timing is inconsistent and depends on individual follow-through. The interval from receipt to completed registration depends on someone remembering to act, and in practice it drifts because registration is a low-priority afterthought amid installation. Registration itself is simple clerical work requiring no special skill, so slowness stems from process neglect rather than difficulty. The task consumes negligible resources, so cost is not the meaningful loss here.

rows.stk: Laboratory managers, equipment custodians, and quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Register sampler and record acquisition metadata
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required purchase, warranty, or assignment information is unavailable during sampler registration
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase, warranty, and ownership details are available when the air-sampling device is added to the records. This helps keep an accurate record of the device and its coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Missing purchase/warranty info during registration is a data-availability ambiguity remote from sampling (relevance low), with a recoverable lookup consequence (severity low), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Acquisition documents are often scattered across emails and systems, so required information is frequently unavailable at registration time. Hunting for missing purchase, warranty, or assignment data interrupts and delays registration. Locating the right documents requires knowing where acquisition records are kept. Unavailable warranty information risks lost coverage, a real cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the field blank cannot be matched to its sampler setup and run record
rows.plain: Make sure the blank sample can be linked to the equipment and record for the test in which it was prepared. This helps show whether any result came from the sample or from the testing process.
rows.imp_rat: Field blank not matchable to its run record is a traceability ambiguity of modest stakes, capped at the mandated 1.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling and chain-of-custody conventions are established, but mismatches still occur when labels are incomplete or transcription is manual, requiring vigilant recordkeeping. Labeling and logging a field blank to its run record takes little time when done at setup. Matching a blank to its sampler and run needs disciplined documentation habits more than special skill. An unmatchable field blank wastes the blank and can invalidate associated data, a modest material and effort loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unstable leak-check readings before a conclusive result is obtained
rows.plain: Get a steady leak-test reading quickly so it is clear whether the equipment leaks. This avoids a long wait before reaching a trustworthy result.
rows.imp_rat: Unstable leak-check readings delay a conclusive result but are ranked below the prior batch, so both proxies remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard leak-check methods usually stabilize and give conclusive results, but noisy or drifting readings still delay closure on some units. Unstable leak-check readings extend the time before a conclusive pass/fail, prolonging setup. Interpreting when a fluctuating reading has settled takes operator judgement and some technique. Repeated leak checks consume little beyond time and minor power.

rows.source_job: Connect power source to air sampler
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the operator waits to access the designated power source
rows.plain: Give the operator quick access to the correct power outlet so the air sampler can be connected.
rows.imp_rat: Time waiting to access a designated power source is a minor logistical delay, peripheral to the collection function (low relevance) with trivially absorbed consequences (low severity), well within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Power availability is generally dependable, so this is a minor, occasional wait rather than a systemic failure. Waiting for a designated power source frees up quickly in most labs but can still cause short queuing delays. Accessing power needs no special skill from the operator. The idle wait costs a little labor time and no material.

rows.source_job: Connect air sampler to data system
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the administrator waits for required network authorization before connection setup
rows.plain: Get the required network permission quickly so the air sampler can be connected without unnecessary waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Admin waiting for network authorization is an IT-access delay peripheral to sampling itself, with trivial, temporary consequences.
rows.sat_rat: IT authorization processes work but are slow and queue-dependent, so the wait recurs predictably. Waiting for network authorization before connection setup can idle the administrator for a notable period in many facilities. The wait is procedural access provisioning, not a skill demand on the person waiting. The delay burns labor time without material cost.
